sources.writer_profile: Gorka Elorrieta is the food and drink editor of Time Out Madrid, where he has curated the publication's restaurant coverage for the Spanish capital. His annual selection of Madrid's best restaurants draws on local knowledge and editorial rigour. Note: Time Out operates Time Out Market Madrid, a commercial food hall venture in the city.
sources.article_title: 30 Best Restaurants in Madrid For 2025, By Local Critics
sources.commercial_conflict_note: Time Out operates Time Out Market Madrid, a commercial food hall.

sources.writer_profile: Paul Richardson is a British food and travel writer who relocated to Spain in 1989. A longtime contributor to Epicurean Ways, he has spent decades documenting Spanish regional cuisine and the Madrid dining scene, approaching the city's restaurants from the perspective of a deeply embedded resident rather than a visitor.
sources.article_title: Paul Richardson's Guide to Eating in Madrid

sources.writer_profile: Lori Zaino is a freelance writer based in Spain whose work appears in The Infatuation and other publications. Her Madrid restaurant guide focuses on accessible entry points to the city's dining scene, spanning neighbourhood tapas bars to established institutions, written for visitors and newcomers rather than specialists.
sources.article_title: Where To Eat When You're Visiting Madrid

sources.writer_profile: David writes Con El Morro Fino, a Madrid-focused gastronomy magazine he runs with Sandra and María. The publication covers restaurants across the city's neighbourhoods with a focus on new openings and hidden-gem discoveries, written by residents with genuine local knowledge and no commercial affiliations.

Mapi Hermida is a food writer for La Gastrónoma, an independent Spanish food publication. Her coverage of Madrid's new restaurant openings tracks the city's dining scene from the perspective of a working food journalist, with particular attention to the first-quarter openings that shape each dining year.
sources.article_title: Los nuevos restaurantes de Madrid en 2026

restaurants.name: VelascoAbellà
restaurants.quote: Their grand personal venture is now up and running, bringing natural light and a refined market-driven tasting menu to Chamartín.

restaurants.name: Ugo Chan
restaurants.quote: The omakase tasting menu features eel and foie gras – a tribute to the great Spanish chef and expert in Basque cooking, Martín Berasategui.

restaurants.name: Desborre
restaurants.quote: Chef Lucía Grávalos serves traditional Anguiano bean stew, which she and her team have managed to modernise with finesse.

restaurants.name: Haramboure
restaurants.quote: A hidden bistro in Salamanca that would not be out of place in Paris, serving Basque-influenced cuisine with precision and restraint.

restaurants.name: OSA
restaurants.quote: Sara Peral and Jorge Muñoz serve just 20 diners per service across two tasting menus, earning Michelin recognition for their focused and personal approach.

restaurants.name: Per Sé Bistro
restaurants.quote: A French-Basque-inspired restaurant with occasional and well-executed Latin touches that elevate the classic bistro formula.

restaurants.name: Desde 1911
restaurants.quote: The menu changes daily following the seasons at this Michelin-starred seafood restaurant whose name honours its founding year.

restaurants.name: Cebo
restaurants.quote: The 11- or 14-course tasting menus feature black grouper, vaca de trabajo beef, and Andine Cornue tomatoes – ingredients chosen for their rarity and precision of flavour.

restaurants.name: Angelita
restaurants.quote: The Villalón brothers' bar on Calle Reina is one of Madrid's most respected natural wine destinations, now closing on weekends.

restaurants.name: Bascoat
restaurants.quote: An urban farmhouse where Basque tradition is refreshed with cosmopolitan touches, earning two Soles Repsol in the 2026 guide.

restaurants.name: El Invernadero
restaurants.quote: Rodrigo de la Calle's Michelin-starred and Green Star-awarded restaurant is focused on gastrobotany – vegetables at the centre of every dish, treated with the rigour of fine dining.

restaurants.name: DiverXO
restaurants.quote: Dabiz Muñoz's punk restaurant with three Michelin stars remains Madrid's most radical dining experience, with tasting menus priced at €450 per person.

restaurants.name: La Tasquita de Enfrente
restaurants.quote: Juanjo López Bedmar's philosophy is daily cooking with a limited number of ingredients – each plate built around the best available product at market that morning.

restaurants.name: Lana
restaurants.quote: Brothers Martín and Joaquín Narvaiz run this Argentine grill with an all-Argentine wine cellar – the most committed South American parrilla in the city.

restaurants.name: Deessa
restaurants.quote: Quique Dacosta's two-Michelin-star restaurant at the Mandarin Oriental Ritz offers some of the most technically ambitious cooking in Madrid.

restaurants.name: Smoked Room
restaurants.quote: Dani García's intimate counter restaurant where smoke is the single most important element – it earned two Michelin stars at once upon opening.

restaurants.name: Corral de la Morería
restaurants.quote: David García's nine-course Gargantúa menu runs alongside the legendary flamenco show, making this one of the few tablao restaurants to earn serious culinary recognition.

restaurants.name: Sacha
restaurants.quote: Sacha Hormaechea's bistro is the favourite restaurant of every Madrid gourmand – an unpretentious room where the tortilla vaga and false lasagne with crab are served without ceremony.

restaurants.name: Tripea
restaurants.quote: At Mercado de Vallehermoso, Tripea serves hot mussel ceviche prepared in a wok – a Peruvian restaurant inside a neighbourhood market that locals treat as their canteen.

restaurants.name: Saddle
restaurants.quote: Saddle's bright, contemporary dining room revives old-world tableside craft – gueridon work, cheese trolley, exacting sauces – without feeling dated, earning a Michelin star and two Soles Repsol.

restaurants.name: Estimar Madrid
restaurants.quote: Chef Rafa Zafra uses only three or four ingredients per dish at this seafood-focused restaurant, where the Mediterranean product speaks for itself.

restaurants.name: La Bien Aparecida
restaurants.quote: José Manuel de Dios focuses on Cantabrian vegetables, including porrusalda – a leek and potato soup – at this quiet Salamanca restaurant that champions northern Spanish produce.

restaurants.name: La Tasquería
restaurants.quote: Javi Estévez's offal-focused restaurant won an unprecedented Michelin star in 2019 and remains one of Madrid's most distinctive propositions – nose-to-tail cooking taken seriously.

restaurants.name: Lakasa
restaurants.quote: César Martín's menu features sirloin Wellington and verdina beans in pepitoria sauce – dishes that balance classical technique with a deep knowledge of Spanish ingredients.

restaurants.name: Trèsde
restaurants.quote: The dining room has neon-coloured acrylic cutlery rests but no tablecloth – a deliberate aesthetic choice that matches the chef's seasonal, boundary-pushing menu.

restaurants.name: Le Bistroman
restaurants.quote: Possibly the best French restaurant in Madrid, serving bouillabaisse and classic bistro cooking with a commitment to the French tradition that few establishments in the city match.

restaurants.name: Noi
restaurants.quote: Gianni Pinto's Italian restaurant serves daring recipes, including tagliatelle with tuna cheek ragù – Italian tradition reworked with Mediterranean ingredients from the Spanish larder.

restaurants.name: Ikigai
restaurants.quote: Yong Wu Nagahira's Japanese fusion cooking at Calle Flor Baja is marked by harmonious flavour combinations and technical precision – a quietly impressive tasting counter.

restaurants.name: Tres por Cuatro
restaurants.quote: Álex Marugán's restaurant features rossejat – a paella-like dish of toasted pasta – alongside a seasonal menu that changes with the best available produce.

restaurants.name: Emma Cocina
restaurants.quote: One of the few tapas bars near Plaza Mayor where locals actually eat – croquetas and wine over long lunches while tourists queue at the Sobrino de Botín next door.

restaurants.name: Bodega de la Ardosa
restaurants.quote: A historic tavern open since 1892, Bodega de la Ardosa is celebrated for its tortilla española – warm, runny-centred, and widely considered the best in Malasaña.

restaurants.name: La Fisna
restaurants.quote: A wine bar in Lavapiés with a 37-page wine list – the kind of place where natural wine lovers settle in for the evening over small plates chosen to match the glass.

restaurants.name: Pez Tortilla
restaurants.quote: A specialist in La Latina dedicated entirely to tortilla de patata – different styles served all day, drawing a loyal neighbourhood crowd that returns for the runny-yolked version.

restaurants.name: Sala de Despiece
restaurants.quote: Set inside a former butcher shop in Chamberí, Sala de Despiece offers theatrical small-plate dining – the butcher's aesthetic intact, the food fully contemporary.

restaurants.name: Lhardy
restaurants.quote: Open since 1839, Lhardy is Madrid's most storied dining institution – a luxury restaurant where cocido madrileño has been served for nearly two centuries in rooms that feel unchanged by time.

restaurants.name: Casa Mingo
restaurants.quote: Casa Mingo is an Asturian cider-house institution near Príncipe Pío – locals have been coming here for roast chicken and sidra on long wooden tables for generations.

restaurants.name: La Venencia
restaurants.quote: A sherry-only bar in Huertas, nearly 100 years old, where the dusty bottles and no-photography rule create one of Madrid's most atmospheric drinking experiences.

restaurants.name: Gofio
restaurants.quote: The Michelin-starred Canarian restaurant in Madrid where chef Safe Cruz brings the island larder – gofio flour, mojo, wrinkled potatoes – to a fine-dining format that won the Premios Metrópoli 2025 Restaurante del Año.

restaurants.name: Malacatín
restaurants.quote: One of Madrid's great traditional restaurants, Malacatín has served cocido madrileño in Lavapiés for decades – a classic that Paul Richardson singles out as essential to understanding the city's culinary identity.

restaurants.name: Casa Lucio
restaurants.quote: Casa Lucio, open since 1974 on Cava Baja, is world-famous for its huevos rotos – fried potatoes and runny eggs that have been served to kings, celebrities, and presidents in its warm tiled rooms.

restaurants.name: Caja de Cerillas
restaurants.quote: Among Paul Richardson's current-trend recommendations for Madrid in 2025 – a small contemporary restaurant earning strong word-of-mouth in the city's growing fine-casual scene.

restaurants.name: Los 33
restaurants.quote: Among the new Salamanca-area restaurants drawing attention in 2025 – Los 33 earned a Repsol Sol and was named Restaurante Revelación at the Premios Metrópoli 2024.

restaurants.name: Arzábal
restaurants.quote: Arzábal faces the Retiro park with a terrace that has drawn diners for over fifteen years – traditional Spanish dishes at tables that feel like an extension of the park itself.

restaurants.name: Taberna Laredo
restaurants.quote: A traditional Madrid taberna in the Ibiza neighbourhood, near Retiro – Taberna Laredo won the 2025 Premio de la Academia Madrileña de Gastronomía for best sala and sommelier.

restaurants.name: Bakko
restaurants.quote: Among the 2025 openings Paul Richardson flags as significant – Bakko combines haute Japanese cuisine with an extensive wine list at a price point that signals serious ambition.

restaurants.name: Zerain
restaurants.quote: One of Madrid's long-standing Basque cider houses, Zerain serves txuleta and traditional sidrería dishes in a setting that replicates the Basque Country experience in the capital.

restaurants.name: Asador Donostiarra
restaurants.quote: A Madrid institution for Basque grilled meats – Asador Donostiarra has served txuleta de buey and pimientos de Gernika to generations of madrileños seeking a piece of the north.

restaurants.name: Casa Julián de Tolosa
restaurants.quote: Casa Julián de Tolosa is Madrid's definitive destination for txuleta de buey – the bone-in beef chop grilled over charcoal in the Tolosa style, served simply with pimientos.

restaurants.name: Ribeira do Miño
restaurants.quote: Ribeira do Miño is a Madrid legend for its mariscada – a shared plateau of Galician shellfish that arrives cold and glistening at tables where the conversation tends to stop.

restaurants.name: Dum Dum
restaurants.quote: Dum Dum en Tetuán sirve dumplings «canallas» que reinterpretan platos conocidos – un local de barrio con precios de entre 20 y 30 euros que desafía las expectativas del dumpling convencional.

restaurants.name: ŌME
restaurants.quote: El primer omakase mexicano de Madrid – en barra y solo para 6 comensales – un formato íntimo en Barrio Salamanca donde la cocina mexicana se eleva a la categoría de alta gastronomía.

restaurants.name: Katsu
restaurants.quote: El auténtico templo del sando y también del donburi en Chamartín – Katsu trae a Madrid el bocadillo japonés más buscado del momento con ingredientes de primera calidad.

restaurants.name: El Pedrusco
restaurants.quote: Tradición castellana con un gran horno de leña en un espectacular asador de Chamberí – El Pedrusco recupera la esencia del asado de carne con técnica artesanal y producto de calidad.

restaurants.name: Tetsu
restaurants.quote: La barra teppanyaki liderada por chefs veinteañeros que está revolucionando Madrid – Tetsu es el culto al hierro y al fuego, con una precisión técnica que desafía los restaurantes japoneses establecidos.

restaurants.name: Batch
restaurants.quote: Alta cocina a precios de mercado, llena de sabor y originalidad en Vallehermoso – Batch ofrece creatividad real sin la barrera económica de la alta gastronomía convencional.

restaurants.name: Caiño
restaurants.quote: Un wine-bar gallego en Calle Ibiza para beber y tapear de maravilla – Caiño es una referencia en la zona de Salamanca-Ibiza para los amantes del vino gallego y los productos de calidad.

restaurants.name: Notablu
restaurants.quote: A new brasserie on Calle Claudio Coello in the heart of Salamanca, Notablu opened in early 2026 with good music and cocktails alongside a full dining menu.

restaurants.name: Obe
restaurants.quote: Lebanese roots and Mediterranean soul in an informal space on Calle del Marqués de Sta. Ana – Obe brings Middle Eastern flavour to Malasaña's increasingly diverse dining scene.

restaurants.name: Keli
restaurants.quote: Spanish gastronomy, casual and approachable on Paseo de la Castellana – Keli opened in late 2025 and recovers the pleasure of unfussy Spanish cooking and the long sobremesa.

restaurants.name: Garelos
restaurants.quote: Garelos opens on Calle Eduardo Dato in 2026, taking over the space formerly occupied by Lúa and bringing Galician cooking to the Chamberí restaurant corridor.

restaurants.name: Aleteo
restaurants.quote: Seafood and fresh fish in an elegant space on Calle de María Molina, run by Grupo Rocacho – Aleteo puts the marine product at the centre, treated with respect and live fire.

restaurants.name: El Campero
restaurants.quote: The first gastronomic space in the world dedicated to red tuna – El Campero brings its Barbate bluefin almadraba tuna temple to María de Molina 25, in a restored palace of over 700 square metres.

restaurants.name: Varra
restaurants.quote: Varra opens on Calle Hermosilla 7 in Salamanca – the group's Madrid expansion following their Formigal success, bringing their contemporary Spanish formula to the capital's most affluent dining district.

restaurants.name: Em Sheriff
restaurants.quote: Authentic Lebanese flavour on Calle Alcalá 54 – Em Sheriff brings the Golden Mile–adjacent location a Middle Eastern menu that aims for the genuine article rather than a tourist approximation.

restaurants.name: Armando
restaurants.quote: An informal space on Calle Carranza 9 where escalope is the absolute protagonist – Armando makes the case for a single, well-executed ingredient as the focus of an entire restaurant concept.

restaurants.name: Olà Beirut
restaurants.quote: A limited-run Lebanese restaurant inside the Hotel Hyatt Regency Hesperia on Paseo de la Castellana 57 – three months in Madrid for this pop-up concept, with no specific dish detail provided.
